summary:
Prophylactic antibiotics significantly lower SSIs for clean-contaminated wounds; most studies indicate rates around 1-2%. Higher reported incidences typically involve greater degrees of contamination not fitting strict 'clean' criteria. To summarize:

Prophylactic antibiotics markedly decrease SSTI incidence from already low base levels seen in clean-contaminated procedures (<5%), generally keeping it under ~1-2%.

The summary captures essential information about reduced risks and typical ranges while addressing potential confounding factors related to degree of contamination. This should effectively convey core insights without extraneous details or assumptions outside provided data sources. Agent
To answer your query regarding the percentage of surgical site infections (SSIs) in patients with clean-contaminated wounds post-prophylactic antibiotic use, multiple studies suggest that such treatment reduces the risk notably but still leaves an occurrence range between roughly 1-2%. These findings are consistent across various literature despite variations noted when dealing with more heavily contaminated cases falling out-of-scope for "clean" classifications. Summarizing succinctly,

Prophylactic antibiotics considerably diminish SSI likelihood among those undergoing clean-contaminated surgery, maintaining their frequency at less than 5% yet often clustering near 1-2%.
